summary: I am now captain of the P.41 fleet, which has South Chicago as its home port. Jack Mc Nellie, a good friend of mine, was initiated as a wheelman many years ago while we were both young sailors. The fleet has been a central part of my maritime life and connections in the Great Lakes region.

I ran away from my home in South Chicago when I was thirteen to become a freshwater sailor. Later, during the winter of 1911, I worked at the Illinois Steel Company in South Chicago before returning to the lakes. South Chicago has always been a significant place in my life, both as home and as a maritime hub.

I have vivid memories of being in Duluth around Thanksgiving when Jack Mc Nellie and I shipped on the yacht Salt Lake City to Brooklyn. Also, I quit my job as a wheelman on a lake boat in Duluth to enlist with the Canadians at the start of World War I. Duluth was an important port and turning point in my early sailing career.

The Salt Lake City was a yacht we took through the Great Lakes to Brooklyn, New York, around Thanksgiving time. It had an open bridge, which made wheelman duties especially tough on Lake Superior in cold, snowy weather. Jack Mc Nellie was assigned to the wheel and endured freezing conditions, but we made it to Brooklyn without major trouble.

Lake Superior is a treacherous lake where I experienced severe weather while on the Salt Lake City and later on the Vulcan. On the Salt Lake City, Jack Mc Nellie faced cold rain, snow, and sleet while steering. On the Vulcan, a heavy snowstorm and crosswinds caused us to drift off course near Whitefish Point, nearly causing disaster.

Brooklyn, New York was our destination when Jack Mc Nellie and I took the yacht Salt Lake City through the Great Lakes. We arrived there after a difficult late-season voyage across Lake Superior and the other lakes. Brooklyn marked the successful end of that challenging trip for us both as young sailors.

When I was thirteen, I stowed away on the Wings of St. Joe, a small sailing vessel running between Benton Harbor, Michigan and South Chicago. It carried fruit and vegetables from Michigan and poultry back to Chicago. On one trip, we were wrecked on a sandbar during a savage gale, but the skipper saved me and the U.S. Coast Guard helped refloat the vessel after the storm.

Benton Harbor, Michigan was one end of the route for the Wings of St. Joe, the small sailing vessel I stowed away on as a boy. We brought fruit and vegetables from Benton Harbor to South Chicago and returned with poultry. It was part of my early sailing experiences on the Great Lakes.

The U.S. Coast Guard came to our aid after the Wings of St. Joe was wrecked on a sandbar near Benton Harbor. They helped float our vessel off the sandbar and brought it safely into port. Their assistance was crucial in saving the ship and ensuring no lives were lost during that savage gale.

Off Whitefish Point in Lake Superior, I had a dangerous experience aboard the Vulcan during a heavy snowstorm. Our skipper tried to navigate between a small rocky island and the mainland, but a crosswind pushed us far off course. We ended up near the Canadian shore instead of the island, narrowly avoiding disaster in that treacherous area.

The Vulcan was my ship during a perilous night off Whitefish Point in Lake Superior. We faced a heavy snowstorm and high winds, and the ship rode high in the water without cargo. Our skipper misjudged the course due to crosswinds, and we drifted far out into the lake, nearly running aground on a rocky island.

During a snowstorm on Lake Superior, we drifted off course and found ourselves near the Canadian shore instead of the intended rocky island. This happened while I was aboard the Vulcan, and it was a frightening moment as the skipper realized our dangerous position. The Canadian shore was a reminder of how easily one can be pushed off course in those conditions.

My entire sailing career has been tied to the Great Lakes, starting when I was thirteen. I sailed on vessels like the Wings of St. Joe, Salt Lake City, and Vulcan across these lakes. The Great Lakes are beautiful but treacherous, especially in late seasons with storms and ice, which halts navigation in winter.

During the winter of 1911, when navigation on the Great Lakes stopped because of ice, I worked at the Illinois Steel Company in South Chicago. I ran an overhead crane and earned more money than I could on a boat. Despite the good pay, the call of the lakes was too strong, and I soon returned to sailing.

At the start of World War I, I was a wheelman on a lake boat but decided to quit in Duluth and enlist with the Canadians. We left Halifax in a transport bound for England with about 5,000 men. During the voyage, our ship was torpedoed by a German submarine, but we managed to beach the vessel and were rescued without loss of life.

Duluth was a key place in my life where I shipped on the Salt Lake City and later quit my wheelman job to enlist for World War I. It was from Duluth that I made the decision to join the Canadian forces. The city marked both an important maritime port and a turning point in my life.

I enlisted with the Canadians at the start of World War I after leaving my job in Duluth. We departed from Halifax, Nova Scotia, bound for England. During the voyage, we were attacked by a German submarine but were saved by beaching the ship and the arrival of destroyers.

Halifax was the port from which we left aboard a transport carrying about 5,000 men to England during World War I. Shortly after leaving Halifax, our ship was torpedoed by a German submarine. Halifax was the last safe harbor before the dangerous Atlantic crossing.

England was our destination after leaving Halifax during World War I. We were on a transport carrying thousands of troops when a German submarine attacked us. Despite the attack, we managed to beach the ship and were rescued, eventually making it to England safely.

A German submarine attacked our transport shortly after we left Halifax during World War I. It fired two torpedoes, hitting our ship and putting us in a sinking condition. Fortunately, we were able to beach the vessel and were later rescued by destroyers, with no loss of life.

After our transport was torpedoed by a German submarine, destroyers came to chase the submarine away and rescued all of us from the damaged ship. Their timely arrival ensured that no lives were lost during this dangerous episode in World War I. The destroyers played a crucial role in our survival.
